Are we sure that there are no legitimate uses of instantiating a new cursor inside `execute`, `executemany` and friends? Because if that's the case we should probably change the assertion depending on whether we're using psycopg2 or psycopg3.

I just went through this from the approach of the 3.1.0 tag's client options (https://github.com/pinterest/pymemcache/tree/v3.1.0/pymemcache/client).

SQLAlchemy got a lot of complaints for *finally* removing "postgres" after 10+ years of warnings that it was deprecated.  Few people saw the warnings, because Python changed the warnings system a while back, and most users no longer see them. You basically have to opt-in to warnings now.

Thinking through this as a user, I could potentially upgrade this library and specify these new options - but then they won't be implemented and things will work unexpected - because I never saw the warnings that I should upgrade.

@zzzeek might feel differently, but I do think submitting 3.5 args against a 3.1 install should be a fatal exception.

LGTM overall (should wait for others to validate the API), but just to make sure I understand the limitations of this:

1. When working with a future representing the return object of a task, future cancellation will not cancel the corresponding task, and we'd need a tighter concurrent.futures integration (e.g. Ray implementing a concurrent.futures executor) in order to support that, right?
2. In the same vein, `future.running()` will always be `False`, even if the task that produces the future is currently running.

Are there any concerns with not supporting the semantics of part of the `concurrent.futures.Future` API? My first impression is that this is fine for now, and that this shouldn't be blocked by a proper `concurrent.Futures` API.

Hello!

I finally had time to run these tests myself. Honestly, all in all the API differences between ancpbids and pybids seem minimal at this point, and I think we can get this done soon.

The main issue I identified were: 

- ancpbids seems to load all derivatives eagerly, even if `derivatives=True` is not set. This actually is fairly important as you may specifically not want to load derivatives
- ancpbids currently uses the "literal" name of entities (e.g. "ses" instead of "session")
- some __repr__s are different and could be improved. Don't need to be identical but it's something to make a decision on
- `get_fieldmap` is not working. Looks like a bug in ancpbids's __getattr__ method

the rest seem like small issue that are very doable to fix, and i've commented on all known test failrues

Thanks for this Yarik, looks like a great start. I have a few opinions/questions/requests...

1) It would be good to document what happens if there is an exception cancelling a task midway through. I'm not sure if that can happen, since I guess it happens in a single DB transaction, so the whole transaction would fail if there was an issue updating one task - is that correct? Is there anything we need to document here about possible partial success cases?
2) I'm not 100% sure we should operate directly on the database. Perhaps there are significant performance improvements with direct DB access, but I'm worried that the two APIs to cancel tasks may diverge in their implementation or introduce subtle differences, especially in regards to how they handle scopes.
3) I tend to think this API call would be better if it _did_ prevent future tasks from being added to the task group. Since a decision task can run for up to 17 minutes after being cancelled, it seems reasonable to me that we should be able to prevent the Queue from allowing tasks to be created with a given taskGroupId, so that that decision task would be ineffective. Without this, it seems quite easy/possible/likely that cancelTaskGroup will often be ineffective and cause frustration since a graph could be cancelled, and then 15 minutes later spawn a large number of tasks.

Although it doesn't currently exist, maybe we should expose it as an API call directly (e.g. `queue.FreezeTaskGroup`), which `queue.cancelTaskGroup` could call under the hood. But e.g. the decision task could also call it in taskgraph, after it finishes adding all tasks, as some offer of safety that the task graph isn't later modified.

Maybe we can discuss this with the broader community in the Taskcluster Community Meeting later today.

I feel comfortable with the correctness given the advisories about usage in the code.

I have a couple things that felt a little odd to me as I was reading, but none that make me want to block this from heading to `experimental` (but might be worth considering if we want to promote this out of experimental at some point):
- In thinking about what we're exposing on the `ScopeGraph`, I'm wondering if we want to lock down the public interface a bit more strictly as it seems possible to fairly easily break the representation by manipulating the public attributes (e.g., naively as a user I might think it safe to call `self.edges.add(foo)` w/o realizing that there is a private attribute that needs to track changes here)
- It's surprising that when I access `ScopeGraph.nodes` I'm not working with `ScopeTreeNodes` (I get that one is `ScopeTree` and one is `ScopeGraph` but we do build a `ScopeGraph` using `ScopeTreeNodes` so it all gets kind of 😵‍💫).

While I haven't thought about it _super_ deeply, I'm wondering if it might make sense to add something like a `ScopeGraph.add_scope()` method that will accept strings or `ScopeTreeNodes` and allow that to manage the properties of the graph so we don't need to leak some of these details, which I think would let us make `nodes` and `edges` private, and make `top_level_scopes` read-only...? But very possible I haven't thought through the downstream consequences sufficiently.

Regardless, this is non-blocking feedback IMO, so I am simultaneously hitting approve.

Overall, things look good, except I'm opposed to the introduction of MediaQueryFeatureFlagDataSource.  I think the specialization of FeatureFlagDataSource into a MediaQuery* and QueryParam* variant adds too much complexity at the moment.  A multi-provider requires introduces a more ambiguity about where a particular feature flag is provided, and all TB surfaces need to include this "QueryParameter provider should appear the last" comment.

imo, the only thing we gain by having this split is that other TB surfaces can opt to include only queryParam flags without including the mediaQuery ones, in a way that does not require composition or redefining all the mediaQuery flags.  This benefit only applies if the other TB surface has no other need to compose/redefine feature flag data source.

If we renamed the existing `QueryParamFeatureFlagDataSource` to be `OSSFeatureFlagDataSource` or something, it would be reasonable for it to read from both query params and media queries.  Other TB surfaces might have to compose/redefine their own feature flag data source, but this is something that other TB surfaces will do already.

Alternatively, wdyt about having DarkModeSupportContainer be responsible for calling `window.matchMedia`?  I don't know if the media query result can change over the lifetime of the page session (if user changes the OS setting without refreshing the tab), but if so, maybe we don't need MediaQueryFeatureFlagDataSource at all?

Will review the code, but first two quick design questions - cc @jtamiace 

## Icon buttons

It's rare in Kolibri (versus Studio) to use purely icon buttons rather than text-based so I wanted to make sure this was intentional. This is partly because learners on tablets don't have tooltips, and partly because learners are less likely to know iconography conventions in contrast to Studio where we use icon buttons more liberally for "power users".

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2367265/134698606-066257ee-2b83-48f4-bd11-3f3f8a979e59.png)

If we do stick with icon buttons, note that we might be using the wrong one for 'delete':

* https://design-system.learningequality.org/icons/#token-clear
* https://design-system.learningequality.org/icons/#token-delete

## Empty states

We've made 'empty states' more consistent, and I think this design deviates slight from previous directions:

- Write some tests to capture the next expectation.

## external vs internal clients

i'm still a bit confused external and internal clients. i understand that we need:

but why does the say "Build and return client for connections **originating** outside LocalStack". Aren't we connect from localstack to an "external" service?

sorry for the stupid question. can you briefly outline the difference and what they are used for? i feel we could use this as an opportunity to create more clarity for other developers as well.

## connect to

from localstack.aws.connect import connect_to
```python
connect_to('sns', source_service='s3', target_arn='arn:aws:sns:ap-south-1:000000000000:lorem')\
.publish(TopicArn='arn:aws:sns:ap-south-1:000000000000:lorem', message='blah')
```

in that case, there would also be a way to generalize the `connect_to` signature, which would give us a way to generalize the client factory interface.

I'm happy to see this making it in. I just need a bit of clarification on some things.

- `eth_utils` has a `ValidationError` that we use in a few places within `web3.py` while in others we use `web3.exceptions.ValidationError`. It looks like at some point there was an idea to use the `eth-utils.exceptions.ValidationError` [across all of the python libraries](https://github.com/ethereum/eth-utils/issues/114). Indeed this isn't used anywhere within the actual `eth-utils` library. Do we want to keep this design? Are there good arguments for or against it? This seems like a good time to revisit this for `web3.py` and it wouldn't be so hard of a change to make. If we think a better design is for each project to have their own `ValidationError` class, do we want to go toward namespace validation errors, as we did with `EthPMValidationError`?

- nit: I slightly prefer the less lengthy `EthPMException` over `PyEthPMException`. The `Py` seems redundant and the module name is `ethpm`. But I can take or leave it. It does make `EthPMValidationError` seem like it needs a `Py` if we keep it... but I prefer both without it.

A few minor edits to the changelog.
LGTM :+1:

FWIW the idea for the initial plugin implementation was to stay close to the previous `conda-*` API and have a similar behavior and specifically not provide an `argparse` specific plugin hook.

That was a design decision to reduce the effort needed for existing packages extending conda with `conda-*` scripts to have a transition path and gain some experience before we support more advanced techniques like supporting click etc.

I've left a few comments and think this still has value FWIW, e.g. we could move this into an own `ArgparseCondaSubcommand` class that could be an “advanced” option for plugins with more complex needs. That said, generally speaking, I would caution us to leaning into `argparse` given its obvious API problems. I'd rather have us work towards supporting tools like [click](https://click.palletsprojects.com/en/8.1.x/) or [typer](https://typer.tiangolo.com/).

after requested changes are made, at minimum, flake8 linter will need to be run, and code changes it recommends need to be made. Running the command `flake8 castle.cms` from the root directory will probably be sufficient, although you may need to pip install flake8 first. There may be errors it complains about that we don't care about. you can look at our specific flake8 config here https://github.com/castlecms/castle.cms/blob/master/setup.cfg, and you can see the automated tests that are run on every commit here: https://github.com/castlecms/castle.cms/blob/master/.travis.yml. you can also run our tests locally before committing with the command `bin/test`, and there are a bunch of config options that sometimes come in handy, which you can find with `bin/test --help`. Also, for the flake8 config, there are slightly different command-line args that can mimic the config in setup.cfg. For example, `flake8 --max-line-length=110 castle.cms`
